pageTitle.value: Rolex Training Centre - A commitment to training

pageCaption.value: Highly qualified employees are essential to create and manufacture quality watches. That is why at Rolex, learning and passing on knowledge are a priority. In 2018, the company opened its Training Centre in Geneva – the culmination of more than 30 years’ commitment to training.

metadescription.value: At Rolex, learning and passing on knowledge are a priority. In 2018, the brand opened its Training Centre in Geneva. Read more on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: Rolex Training Centre - A commitment to training

title.value: A Culture of Training and Mentorship

text.value: Highly qualified employees are essential for creating and manufacturing high-quality watches. That is why at Rolex, learning and knowledge transfer are a priority. In 2018, the company introduced a dedicated service for this: the Rolex Training Centre, culminating more than 30 years’ commitment to skills acquisition and development.

Mastering skills, acquiring and sharing knowledge, along with the continuous quest for perfection are key aspects of training at Rolex. For several decades, the company has been creating training programmes and methods to suit its various professions and support its employees’ career development. From initial training to continuing on-the-job tuition and specialist courses, a considerable range of resources are available. They enable the brand to pass on its carefully curated heritage of skills, practices and Rolex-specific knowledge.


Rolex’s investment in training – which goes beyond the scope of watchmaking and benefits all sectors – has three objectives: to ensure progress, allow skills to be developed within the company, and perpetuate the company’s unique culture. Rolex offers the same training opportunities to all its employees, whether they are based in Geneva, home to the brand’s world headquarters and three production sites, in Bienne, where the movements are made, or in the canton of Fribourg, where the brand is developing its industrial operations. All told, nearly 12,000 square metres of space is devoted to training facilities in Geneva, Bienne and Fribourg, with a sole objective: to train more than 10,000 employees, managers, apprentices and interns each year.

In 1984, Rolex was the first company in Geneva to take on watchmaking apprentices. In 1989, the brand opened its doors to its first apprentices in Bienne. Since then, it has trained hundreds of people of different ages and backgrounds. Apprentices are selected following a rigorous recruitment process that evaluates their intellectual and manual abilities, as well as their soft skills. In time, Rolex aims to provide ongoing initial training to 500 apprentices in the fields of watchmaking, industry and services.
In 1984, Rolex was the first company in Geneva to take on watchmaking apprentices.

The training of these future professionals combines periods of practical training within the company and theoretical classes in a vocational college each week – in line with the legal framework governing apprenticeships, which are very popular in Switzerland. The importance Rolex places on this “dual” education system and its evolution has led the brand to become actively involved with relevant official governing bodies.


In all, apprenticeships for more than 20 professions are offered to young people between the ages of 15 and 25 each year.

title.value: The training of these future professionals combines periods of practical training within the company and theoretical classes.

text.value: In order to provide apprentices and employees with the best training conditions, the brand opened the Rolex Training Centre in September 2018. The service centralizes Rolex’s key training expertise in every region where the brand operates. Designed as places of interaction with open, versatile layouts, the training facilities encourage different generations to come together to optimize the transmission of knowledge and progression of skills. They are firmly focused on the future; thanks to their monitoring activities, they are able to constantly incorporate evolutions in the teaching, technological, sociological, organizational and legislative sectors. Spearheading these knowledge-sharing hubs is the facility in Geneva, which boasts a contemporary design reflected in its interior furnishings. It is composed of some 20 spaces spread over 5,500 square metres and is equipped with cutting-edge educational tools.

Rolex’s training facilities provide, on an ongoing basis, dedicated courses for apprentices and continuous training modules that teach employees about the company’s history, culture and values, as well as its watches. They also dispense all other Rolex training programmes available to employees over the course of their careers, from the acquisition and development of technical expertise to management skills. Through engaging and dynamic teaching methods, trainers from both inside and outside the company consolidate and guarantee the passage of vital knowledge between generations. Internships are also offered to students in higher education, with Rolex taking on around 150 interns each year.

title.value: The human at the heart of the company

text.value: Hans Wilsdorf, the founder of Rolex, recognized the importance of surrounding oneself with highly qualified employees. He was also convinced that their well-being would directly contribute to the success of the company. This open-mindedness and ability to place the person at the heart of the business still play an intrinsic role in Rolex’s company culture to this day. The brand’s tangible, significant commitment to training and to the integration of the next generation into the professional world are testimony to this concept.

title.value: This open-mindedness and ability to place the person at the heart of the business still play an intrinsic role in Rolex’s company culture to this day.

pageCaption.value: Faced with a shortage of skilled watchmakers in the United States, Rolex built a school in 2001 to train a new generation of young specialists to service high-quality mechanical wristwatches.

metadescription.value: Faced with a shortage of skilled watchmakers in the United States, Rolex built a school in 2001 to train a new generation of young specialists to service high-quality mechanical wristwatches.

pageTitle.value: The man behind the crown - Hans Wilsdorf - Perpetual

pageCaption.value: Everything about Rolex carries the stamp of one exceptional human being. Hans Wilsdorf, the founder of Rolex, was not just a visionary entrepreneur whose stream of inventions, such as the first waterproof wristwatch, changed the world. He was an altruist, whose enduring philanthropy has left a mark that goes far beyond watchmaking.

pageTitle.value: Sir Francis Chichester - Time on the high seas

pageCaption.value: Few characterize the daring spirit that has marked the history of yachting better than Sir Francis Chichester, whose single-handed round-the-world voyage in 1966–1967 charted the course for subsequent generations of yachtsmen and yachtswomen.
